body.front: The prefix brady- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: bradycardia = slow heart rate

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The prefix tachy- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: tachycardia = fast heart rate

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The prefix hyper- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: hypertension = above normal blood pressure

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The prefix hypo- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: hypotension = below normal blood pressure

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -emia refers to a {{blank}} condition.
body.hint: Think: anemia = without normal blood cells; hypokalemia = low potassium in blood

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -itis means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: appendicitis = inflammation of the appendix

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -ectomy means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: appendectomy = surgical removal of the appendix

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -ostomy means creation of a {{blank}}.
body.back: permanent opening (stoma) into a hollow organ or body cavity
body.hint: Think: colostomy = opening into the colon; tracheostomy = opening into the trachea

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.prompt: Define bradycardia. What is the threshold heart rate?
rubric: Threshold: < 60 beats per minute in adults
body.modelAnswer: Bradycardia is a heart rate below 60 beats per minute in an adult at rest.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.prompt: Define tachycardia. What is the threshold heart rate?
rubric: Threshold: > 100 beats per minute in adults
body.modelAnswer: Tachycardia is a heart rate above 100 beats per minute in an adult at rest.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: According to the 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines, Stage 1 hypertension begins at a blood pressure of:
body.explanation: The 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines define Stage 1 hypertension as ≥130 systolic OR ≥80 diastolic. The previous threshold of 140/90 is now Stage 2.

sourceCitation: 2017 ACC/AHA High Blood Pressure Guidelines; Saunders NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.stem: Hypotension in adults is generally defined as a systolic blood pressure of:
body.explanation: Hypotension is defined as systolic BP < 90 mmHg (or a drop ≥ 20 mmHg from baseline). Clinical context always matters — what is hypotensive for one patient may be normal for another.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.front: Diaphoresis refers to {{blank}} sweating, often associated with pain, shock, fever, or medication effects.
body.hint: From Greek: dia- (through) + phorein (to carry) — literally 'sweating through'

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: The prefix dys- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: dyspnea = difficult breathing; dysphagia = difficulty swallowing

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The prefix a- or an- means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: apnea = without breathing; anemia = without enough red blood cells

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -pnea relates to {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: dyspnea, apnea, tachypnea, orthopnea — all involve breathing

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -cardia refers to a condition of the {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: bradycardia = slow heart rate; tachycardia = fast heart rate

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: The suffix -uria refers to a condition of the {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: hematuria = blood in urine; polyuria = excessive urine

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.prompt: Define edema. Name two common causes in nursing practice.
rubric: Abnormal accumulation of fluid in interstitial (tissue) spaces
Two causes from: heart failure, hypoalbuminemia, venous insufficiency, lymphatic obstruction, inflammation, medications (e.g. calcium channel blockers)
body.modelAnswer: Edema is the abnormal accumulation of excess fluid in the interstitial spaces of body tissues. Common causes include heart failure (increased hydrostatic pressure) and hypoalbuminemia (decreased oncotic pressure).

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Cyanosis is a {{blank}} discoloration of the skin and mucous membranes caused by reduced oxygen saturation.
body.hint: Central cyanosis (lips, tongue) indicates lower SpO2 than peripheral cyanosis (fingernails)

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Orthopnea is difficulty breathing that is relieved by {{blank}}.
body.back: sitting upright or elevating the head of the bed
body.hint: ortho- = straight/upright; seen in heart failure and severe COPD

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.prompt: Differentiate dyspnea from orthopnea. What underlying condition is commonly associated with both?
rubric: Dyspnea: subjective sensation of difficult or labored breathing
Orthopnea: dyspnea that worsens when supine and improves when upright
Common association: left-sided heart failure / pulmonary edema
body.modelAnswer: Dyspnea is the subjective experience of difficult or uncomfortable breathing. Orthopnea is a type of dyspnea that worsens in the recumbent position and is relieved by sitting up. Both are hallmark signs of left-sided heart failure due to pulmonary venous congestion.

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.front: Apnea is defined as a temporary {{blank}} of breathing lasting at least 10 seconds.
body.hint: a- (without) + -pnea (breathing); central apnea vs. obstructive sleep apnea

sourceCitation: Fundamentals of Nursing, 9th ed., Potter & Perry

body.front: Hematuria refers to the presence of {{blank}} in the urine.
body.hint: hemat/o = blood; -uria = urine condition

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner

body.front: Polyuria refers to the excretion of {{blank}} amounts of urine, commonly associated with diabetes mellitus or diabetes insipidus.
body.hint: poly- = many/much; -uria = urine; definition often cites > 3 L/day

sourceCitation: Saunders Comprehensive Review for the NCLEX-RN, 9th ed.

body.front: The suffix -logy means {{blank}}.
body.hint: Think: cardiology = study of the heart; neurology = study of the nervous system

sourceCitation: Medical Terminology: A Short Course, 8th ed., Chabner
